name: C#
description: Microsoft's elegant OOP language powering .NET apps, game development with Unity, and enterprise software.

lessons.title: Hello World & Console
lessons.content: C# programs are organized into namespaces and classes. The Main method is the entry point. Console.WriteLine() outputs text to the console. C# is strongly typed and runs on the .NET runtime.

exercises.question: Which method prints a line to the console in C#?
hints: It is part of the System.Console class

exercises.question: What prefix creates an interpolated string in C#?

lessons.title: Classes & Properties
lessons.content: C# classes support properties — special members that expose private fields with get/set accessors. Auto-properties generate the backing field automatically. This is a core C# pattern.

exercises.question: What does 'public int Age { get; private set; }' mean?
options: Age can be read and written from anywhere
Age can be read from anywhere but only set inside the class
Age is only accessible inside the class
exercises.answer: Age can be read from anywhere but only set inside the class
hints: private set restricts writing to within the class

lessons.title: LINQ — Language Integrated Query
lessons.content: LINQ lets you query collections with SQL-like syntax directly in C#. Use .Where() to filter, .Select() to transform, .OrderBy() to sort, and .FirstOrDefault() to get one element.

exercises.question: Which LINQ method filters elements based on a condition?
hints: Like SQL WHERE — it keeps only matching elements

lessons.title: Async / Await
lessons.content: C# async/await makes asynchronous programming straightforward. Mark a method with 'async', use 'await' on Tasks, and the compiler handles the rest. This keeps UIs responsive and servers efficient.

exercises.question: What return type should an async method that returns a string use?
hints: async methods wrap their return type in Task<>
